title: Acid-Base Interpretation: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Apply a systematic approach to ABG interpretation
Calculate anion gap and identify causes of HAGMA

sections.title: The Basics: pH, PaCO2, and HCO3
sections.content:
**Normal Values:**
• pH: 7.35-7.45
• PaCO2: 35-45 mmHg
• HCO3: 22-26 mEq/L

**The Henderson-Hasselbalch Relationship:**
pH ∝ HCO3 / PaCO2

**Key Concept:**
• HCO3 is controlled by the KIDNEYS (metabolic)
• PaCO2 is controlled by the LUNGS (respiratory)
• When one system causes a problem, the other compensates

sections.title: Step 1: Acidemia or Alkalemia?
sections.content:
Look at the pH first:

• **pH < 7.35 = Acidemia** (the blood is too acidic)
• **pH > 7.45 = Alkalemia** (the blood is too alkaline)
• **pH 7.35-7.45** = Normal or compensated

**Clinical Pearl:**
The body NEVER overcompensates. If pH is low, there must be an acidosis present. If pH is high, there must be an alkalosis present.

sections.title: Step 2: Respiratory or Metabolic?
sections.content:
**Which system is causing the primary problem?**

Look at PaCO2 and HCO3:

**If pH is LOW (acidemia):**
• High PaCO2 → Respiratory acidosis
• Low HCO3 → Metabolic acidosis

**If pH is HIGH (alkalemia):**
• Low PaCO2 → Respiratory alkalosis
• High HCO3 → Metabolic alkalosis

**Memory Aid:**
• CO2 is an acid (carbonic acid)
• HCO3 is a base (bicarbonate buffer)

sections.title: Step 3: Is Compensation Appropriate?
sections.content:
The body always tries to compensate. Use these formulas:

**Metabolic Acidosis:**
Expected PaCO2 = 1.5 × HCO3 + 8 (±2) — Winter's Formula
OR: PaCO2 should ≈ last 2 digits of pH

**Metabolic Alkalosis:**
Expected PaCO2 = 0.7 × HCO3 + 21 (±2)

**Respiratory Acidosis:**
• Acute: HCO3 increases 1 for every 10 rise in PaCO2
• Chronic: HCO3 increases 3.5 for every 10 rise in PaCO2

**Respiratory Alkalosis:**
• Acute: HCO3 decreases 2 for every 10 fall in PaCO2
• Chronic: HCO3 decreases 5 for every 10 fall in PaCO2

**If compensation doesn't match → Mixed disorder!**

sections.title: Step 4: Calculate the Anion Gap
sections.content:
**Formula:**
Anion Gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3)

**Normal:** 8-12 mEq/L (varies by lab)

**High Anion Gap Metabolic Acidosis (HAGMA):**
Anion gap > 12 suggests unmeasured anions

**MUDPILES Causes:**
• **M**ethanol
• **U**remia
• **D**KA (and starvation ketosis)
• **P**ropylene glycol, Paraldehyde
• **I**soniazid, Iron
• **L**actate (shock, sepsis, seizures)
• **E**thylene glycol
• **S**alicylates

**Non-Anion Gap (Hyperchloremic) Metabolic Acidosis:**
• Diarrhea (GI HCO3 loss)
• RTA (renal tubular acidosis)
• Normal saline infusion
• Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors

sections.title: Step 5: Calculate the Delta-Delta (Δ/Δ)
sections.content:
**When you have HAGMA, check if there's another metabolic disorder hiding!**

**Delta Ratio = (AG - 12) / (24 - HCO3)**

Interpretation:
• **Ratio < 1:** HAGMA + non-gap metabolic acidosis
• **Ratio 1-2:** Pure HAGMA
• **Ratio > 2:** HAGMA + metabolic alkalosis

**Example:**
Patient with DKA (HAGMA) who is also vomiting (metabolic alkalosis):
• AG = 28, HCO3 = 18
• Delta ratio = (28-12)/(24-18) = 16/6 = 2.7 → Mixed HAGMA + metabolic alkalosis

sections.title: Common Clinical Scenarios
sections.content:
**1. DKA:**
• Primary: HAGMA (ketoacids)
• Expected: Respiratory compensation (Kussmaul breathing)
• Watch for: Vomiting causing concomitant metabolic alkalosis

**2. COPD Exacerbation:**
• Primary: Respiratory acidosis (CO2 retention)
• Chronic: Metabolic compensation (elevated HCO3)
• Acute-on-chronic: pH drops despite elevated HCO3

**3. Septic Shock:**
• Early: Respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation)
• Late: HAGMA (lactic acidosis)
• May see mixed picture

**4. Aspirin Overdose:**
• Classic mixed disorder: Respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA
• Salicylates stimulate respiratory center AND cause HAGMA

sections.title: The 5-Step Approach Summary
sections.content:
**1. pH → Acidemia or Alkalemia?**

**2. Primary disorder → Respiratory or Metabolic?**
(Match the abnormal value to the pH direction)

**3. Compensation → Appropriate or not?**
(Use formulas; if not appropriate → mixed disorder)

**4. Anion Gap → HAGMA?**
(If metabolic acidosis, always calculate AG)

**5. Delta-Delta → Hidden disorder?**
(If HAGMA, check for additional metabolic process)

**Always correlate with clinical picture!**

keyTakeaways: Always start with pH to determine acidemia vs alkalemia
Match the primary disorder to the pH direction
The body never overcompensates—look for mixed disorders
Calculate anion gap in all metabolic acidosis cases
Use delta-delta ratio to find hidden metabolic disorders in HAGMA
Correlate ABG findings with clinical presentation

references.citation: Berend K, de Vries APJ, Gans ROB. Physiological Approach to Assessment of Acid-Base Disturbances. N Engl J Med. 2014;371:1434-1445
Kraut JA, Madias NE. Metabolic Acidosis: Pathophysiology, Diagnosis and Management. Nat Rev Nephrol. 2010;6:274-285
references.keyContent: Comprehensive review of metabolic acidosis causes and treatment

references.citation: Adrogue HJ, Madias NE. Secondary Responses to Altered Acid-Base Status. J Am Soc Nephrol. 2010;21:920-923

references.title: Approach to Acid-Base Disorders: A Practical Review

reviewQuestions.question: ABG shows: pH 7.28, PaCO2 24, HCO3 11, Na 140, Cl 100. What is the primary disorder?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Low pH = acidemia. Low HCO3 = metabolic acidosis. Expected PaCO2 by Winter's formula = 1.5(11) + 8 = 24.5 ± 2. Actual PaCO2 is 24—appropriate compensation. AG = 140 - (100+11) = 29 → HAGMA.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with COPD has: pH 7.36, PaCO2 60, HCO3 33. What does this represent?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Normal pH despite high CO2 indicates chronic compensation. For chronic respiratory acidosis, expect HCO3 to rise 3.5 per 10 CO2 rise. Expected HCO3 = 24 + 3.5(2) = 31. Actual is 33—consistent with chronic compensated respiratory acidosis.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient has HAGMA with AG of 30 and HCO3 of 20. The delta ratio is:
reviewQuestions.explanation: Delta ratio = (30-12)/(24-20) = 18/4 = 4.5. Ratio > 2 indicates HAGMA with concomitant metabolic alkalosis (HCO3 higher than expected for degree of gap).

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected acid base interpretation?

options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in acid base interpretation. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination

coachDx.misconception: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Obtain a thorough history and physical examination from Consult a specialist before any workup.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of acid base interpretation. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically

coachDx.misconception: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Consider the differential diagnosis systematically from Order every available test to rule out all possibilities.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.

mcqs.stem: When managing acid base interpretation, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values

coachDx.misconception: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values from Always defer to the most recent published study.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
